description:
You are an infrastructure engineer asked to do a very small-scale, but critical, performance-optimization step in an existing provisioning script.

Current situation  
• Two byte-for-byte identical 256 KiB “golden” image files exist:  
 /home/user/images/primary/golden.img  
 /home/user/images/backup/golden.img  

• Having two physical copies wastes I/O bandwidth during backups and consumes extra space.  
• The quickest fix is to replace the backup copy with a hard link that points at the primary copy (same inode).

Your job  
1. Replace /home/user/images/backup/golden.img with a hard link that points to /home/user/images/primary/golden.img.  
   • After the change both paths **must share the exact same inode number**.  
   • The data of the image file must remain intact (no truncation, no corruption).  
2. Produce an audit log at /home/user/images/link_optimization.log so the CI pipeline can automatically verify success.

Required audit-log format (4 lines, **exact spelling**, NO extra spaces except where shown):  
Line-1: TIMESTAMP: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS  ← local system time at the moment you finish linking  
Line-2: PRIMARY_INODE: <numeric-inode-value>  
Line-3: BACKUP_INODE: <numeric-inode-value>  
Line-4: STATUS: linked

Additional rules the test-suite will check  
• The file /home/user/images/link_optimization.log must exist and contain exactly four newline-terminated lines.  
• The numeric inode value on lines 2 and 3 must be identical.  
• Line 4 must read exactly “STATUS: linked”.  
• /home/user/images/backup/golden.img must be a hard link to /home/user/images/primary/golden.img (not a symlink, not a copy).  

Hints (do NOT display commands here)  
• The classic way to produce a hard link is a single ln invocation.  
• You can fetch an inode number with stat.  
• Standard shell tools such as date can format timestamps.  

This change generally needs only two or three well-chosen shell commands. Work inside the running terminal; no root privileges are required.
truth:
# Pre-existing file structure before the agent starts
Directory /home/user/images/primary exists with:
    /home/user/images/primary/golden.img  →  binary file, exactly 262 144 bytes (256 KiB), inode X (unknown until runtime)

Directory /home/user/images/backup exists with:
    /home/user/images/backup/golden.img   →  exact byte-for-byte copy of the primary file, inode Y ≠ X (they are different copies at start)

The automated checker will:
1. Confirm that after the agent finishes,
       stat -c '%i' /home/user/images/primary/golden.img
   is identical to
       stat -c '%i' /home/user/images/backup/golden.img

2. Read /home/user/images/link_optimization.log and parse:
       TIMESTAMP: <regex ^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}$>
       PRIMARY_INODE: <numeric same as #1>
       BACKUP_INODE:  <numeric same as #1>
       STATUS: linked

3. Fail if any extra characters, extra lines, or spacing deviations are found in the log.

The checker ignores the actual timestamp value but validates its format.

Permissions:
• All directories under /home/user are owned by the unprivileged “user” account that the agent runs as.
• The agent therefore has full read/write rights to create hard links and write the log file.

No other hidden files or processes are involved.
